FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study cohort (N=9,208), baseline ages 9-10 years. Nagata et al. 2023 prospective cohort analysis.

FT_IC2.evidence: Total screen time plus specific modalities examined: television/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social networking. All qualifying smartphone/video variables. Applies B1 (separate coefficients for multiple modalities).

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analysis (Models 1-3). Adjusted odds ratios (AOR) with 95% CI reported for each screen modality predicting OCD at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients in Table 3: OR, AOR, 95% CI for each screen modality. Example: video games AOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28), p=0.015.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with multiple screen time variables predicting OCD diagnosis (KSADS-5 assessed outcome).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article (Journal of Adolescent Health 2023), no evidence of preprint/duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (smartphone/social media) is the INDEPENDENT variable (exposure), not dependent variable. Outcome is OCD diagnosis. Fails condition (a) of FT-EC7 binding rule.
notes: Prospective design. Each hour of video games and watching videos prospectively associated with OCD. Adjusted models control for depression, family history of psychopathology, and demographic variables.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (N=10,280), Year 2 data, ages 10-14 years. Nagata et al. 2023.

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use behaviors: television/streaming, video games, music, phone (talking/texting), social media, chat rooms, internet browsing, device in bedroom, phone ringer activated. Qualifies as smartphone/social media use (B1, multiple distinct modalities with separate coefficients).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analyses (adjusted relative risk ratios, ARR). Each bedtime screen behavior analyzed with ARR and 95% CI reported for sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients in Table 3 and text: ARR values with 95% CI. Example: device in bedroom ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44); phone ringer on ARR for trouble falling/staying asleep reported.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD cross-sectional empirical analysis at Year 2 with screen use variables predicting sleep disturbance outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article (Sleep Health 2023, 9:497-502), no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/bedtime screen behaviors are the INDEPENDENT variable, not dependent. Sleep disturbance is outcome. Fails condition (a) of FT-EC7 binding rule. Additionally, cross-sectional design violates condition (c).
notes: Cross-sectional data from Year 2. Multiple bedtime screen modalities examined (texting, video chat, social media, videos, games). Adjustments include depression, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, data collection period (COVID).

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (N=11,875), baseline ages 9-11 years. Nagata et al. 2023 prospective cohort study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time modalities: total screen time, television/movies, video games, social media, video chat, texting. All qualify as smartphone/video/social media use (B1: separate coefficients for multiple modalities reported).

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses. Prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% CI reported for each modality predicting conduct disorder or oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients in text and tables: PR values with 95% CI. Example: social media PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for conduct disorder; video games PR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.21) for oppositional defiant disorder.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD prospective empirical analysis with screen time variables predicting disruptive behavior disorder diagnoses (KSADS-5).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article (Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry 2023, 64(1):125-135), no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/social media is the INDEPENDENT variable, not dependent variable. Outcomes are conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder diagnoses. Fails condition (a) of FT-EC7 binding rule.
notes: Prospective design with 1-year follow-up. Strongest association between social media and conduct disorder (62% higher prevalence per hour). Exposure to >4 hours total screen time per day associated with higher prevalence of both disruptive behavior disorders. Modified Poisson regression used.
